title: End-to-End Training for Unified Tokenization and Latent Denoising

headings.text: Beyond Vision: Application to Domains Without Pretrained Encoders

section_anchors.text: We propose UNITE – an autoencoder architecture for unified tokenization and latent diffusion.
The shared parameters enable gradients to jointly shape the latent space, encouraging a common latent language.
This separation is convenient, but it departs from the principle of end-to-end learning
We propose an alternative perspective on end-to-end training.
we focus on a single-stage training approach that learns tokenization and generation jointly, without access to pretrained SSL encoders.
UNITE replaces the separate tokenizer and latent denoiser with a shared set of parameters, the Generative Encoder,

Training Pipeline: We adopt a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone for both the generative encoder and the decoder, motivated by the strong empirical performance of Transformer architectures in diffusion/flow denoising.
The total objective is the sum of reconstruction and generation losses.
The adversarial nature of joint training.
In UNITE, we pursue end-to-end training by sharing parameters between the encoder and denoiser roles of a single network.
Can a single training job produce both a strong tokenizer and a strong generator?
UNITE-B reaches an FID of 2.12, substantially improving over the single-stage baseline JiT-B/16
We demonstrate this capability on QM9 molecule generation, a setting with no DINO-equivalent pretrained encoder.
section_anchors.expected_section: Beyond Vision: Application to Domains Without Pretrained Encoders

section_anchors.text: We report total training FLOPs measured with gradient checkpointing enabled.
We present UNITE, a unified approach to joint tokenization and generation.
The core contribution of UNITE is to align tokenization and generation by training both over a shared latent space.
In this appendix, we first provide more details on the reconstruction fidelity results in Sec. A.
Here, we provide additional details on the adversarial fine-tuning procedure that reduces rFID from 1.01 to 0.51, without changing gFID.
For reproducibility, we detail the full evaluation protocol used for all ImageNet-256 generation results.
The QM9 dataset (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014) contains approximately 130K stable small organic molecules with up to 9 heavy atoms from the set {C, N, O, F}.
The MP20 dataset from the Materials Project (Jain et al., 2013) contains 45,231 inorganic crystal structures with up to 20 atoms per unit cell.
we provide a few additional ablations that further improve UNITE’s reconstruction and generation fidelity.
Tab. 7 and Tab. 8 provide additional architectural and training details.
